cases.response_preview: These four inner planets are rocky because they formed close to the Sun, where temperatures were too high for ices like water and ammonia to condense. As a result, only silicate rocks could form in their cores.

body.npc_response: These four inner planets are rocky because they formed close to the Sun, where temperatures were too high for ices like water and ammonia to condense. As a result, only silicate rocks could form in their cores.

cases.response_preview: An astronomical unit, or AU for short, is a standard distance measure used in our Solar System. On average, Earth's orbit around the Sun spans about 1 AU. cases.response_preview: Asteroids and comets are remnants from the early Solar System, providing clues about its formation. They contain primitive materials that have been preserved since the time of the planetesimal disk, offering insights into the conditions under which planets formed.

body.npc_response: Asteroids and comets are remnants from the early Solar System, providing clues about its formation. They contain primitive materials that have been preserved since the time of the planetesimal disk, offering insights into the conditions under which planets formed.

cases.memory_context_preview:
Player: Please remember that I am most curious about Jupiter's Great Red Spot and why it has lasted so long.
NPC: The Great Red Spot is a persistent anticyclonic storm on Jupiter, which has been raging for at least 187 years. It's larger than Earth in diameter and has winds that can reach speeds of up to 400 miles per hour, making it one of the most ex
cases.recall_response_preview: Jupiter's Great Red Spot. It has been raging for at least 187 years, larger than Earth in diameter and high enough to swallow several of our planets whole, with winds reaching speeds up to 400 miles per hour.

body.npc_response: The Great Red Spot is a persistent anticyclonic storm on Jupiter, which has been raging for at least 187 years. It's larger than Earth in diameter and has winds that can reach speeds of up to 400 miles per hour, making it one of the most extreme weather phenomena in our Solar System.
